codeSnapshot.label: bertscore.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
from bert_score import score as bertscore
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel

# BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2020)
# Token-level semantic similarity using contextual BERT embeddings
# P = (1/|ŷ|) Σ_{x̂∈ŷ} max_{x∈y} cos(x̂, x)
# R = (1/|y|) Σ_{x∈y} max_{x̂∈ŷ} cos(x̂, x)
# F1 = 2PR/(P+R)

# Quick usage with bert_score library
candidates = [
    'The cat is on the mat.',
    'A dog ran quickly through the park.',
]
references = [
    'There is a cat on the mat.',
    'The dog sprinted across the park.',
]

# Returns precision, recall, F1 tensors (one value per example)
P, R, F1 = bertscore(candidates, references, lang='en', verbose=False,
                      model_type='microsoft/deberta-xlarge-mnli')  # best model
for i, (p, r, f) in enumerate(zip(P, R, F1)):
    print(f'Example {i}: P={p:.3f} R={r:.3f} F1={f:.3f}')

# Manual computation for understanding
def bertscore_manual(candidate_tokens, reference_tokens, model, tokenizer):
    """Compute BERTScore F1 between one candidate and one reference."""
    def get_embeddings(tokens):
        encoded = tokenizer(tokens, return_tensors='pt', is_split_into_words=True,
                           add_special_tokens=False, padding=True, truncation=True)
        with torch.no_grad():
            output = model(**encoded).last_hidden_state[0]  # (T, H)
        # Normalize to unit vectors (for cosine similarity)
        return output / output.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)

    c_embs = get_embeddings(candidate_tokens)  # (|c|, H)
    r_embs = get_embeddings(reference_tokens)   # (|r|, H)
    # Cosine similarity matrix: sim[i,j] = cos(c_embs[i], r_embs[j])
    sim = c_embs @ r_embs.T  # (|c|, |r|)
    # Precision: for each candidate token, max similarity to any reference token
    precision = sim.max(dim=1).values.mean().item()
    # Recall: for each reference token, max similarity to any candidate token
    recall = sim.max(dim=0).values.mean().item()
    f1 = 2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall + 1e-9)
    return {'precision': precision, 'recall': recall, 'f1': f1}

eli3: BERTScore measures how semantically similar two texts are using BERT's deep language understanding. Instead of checking if exact words match (like BLEU/ROUGE), it converts each word into a meaning vector and compares those vectors. Synonyms and paraphrases that mean the same thing score highly.

For each word in the candidate, BERTScore finds the most similar word in the reference (by cosine similarity of their BERT embeddings). Precision averages these best matches for candidate words; recall averages them for reference words. This handles paraphrasing that pure n-gram metrics miss.

BERTScore correlates much better with human judgments than BLEU and ROUGE, especially for abstractive generation where the model rewrites content in new words. It's now the preferred automatic metric for text summarisation and generation quality assessment.

whenWhy.title: Evaluating abstractive text generation
whenWhy.body: Abstractive summarisation and paraphrase generation produce outputs that are semantically equivalent to references but use different words — BLEU and ROUGE penalise this unfairly. BERTScore gives credit for semantic similarity: 'automobile' and 'car' are embedded near each other in BERT space (cosine similarity ~0.95). Zhang et al. (2020) showed BERTScore correlates significantly better with human judgments on WMT MT, CNN/DailyMail summarisation, and dialogue evaluation tasks.

whenWhy.title: LLM output quality assessment
whenWhy.body: BERTScore is commonly used to evaluate LLM outputs for tasks with reference answers: instruction following, question answering, dialogue. It captures semantic adequacy — does the candidate express the same meaning as the reference? — better than lexical metrics. In multi-reference settings, BERTScore is computed between the candidate and each reference, then the maximum F1 is taken. Many recent LLM evaluation papers (HELM, LMSYS Arena) report BERTScore alongside ROUGE and human eval.

whenWhy.title: Model selection for BERTScore computation
whenWhy.body: The choice of BERT backbone matters significantly for BERTScore quality. Zhang et al. (2020) benchmark: DeBERTa-xlarge-mnli achieves the highest human correlation for English. Multilingual evaluation: mBERT or XLM-RoBERTa. The bert_score library provides a built-in model recommendation per language and task. Larger models generally give better scores but are slower — DeBERTa-xlarge requires 2GB GPU memory. For fast evaluation: bert-base-uncased is sufficient; for publication: use deberta-xlarge-mnli.

deepDiveIntro: BERTScore works by converting every token in the candidate and reference into a contextual embedding using a pre-trained language model. Precision is computed by matching each candidate token to the most similar reference token (by cosine similarity) and averaging those scores. Recall does the same in reverse. IDF weighting gives rare, informative words more influence in the average than common stopwords. [1]

Because the embeddings are contextual, the same word in different sentences has different representations. This means BERTScore is sensitive to word order and meaning in context — 'the dog bit the man' and 'the man bit the dog' score differently even though they share all the same words. The intermediate layers of BERT (around layers 8-9) tend to work best for scoring rather than the final layer, which is too specialised. [1]

deepDive.heading: Precision, Recall, and F1 Computation
deepDive.body: For precision, each candidate token is matched to its closest reference token by cosine similarity, and those best-match scores are averaged. For recall, the matching runs the other way — each reference token is matched to its closest candidate token. F1 is the harmonic mean of the two. The 'greedy matching' design — take the best single match for each token rather than finding an optimal global alignment — runs efficiently in time proportional to the number of token pairs. IDF weighting down-weights common tokens like 'the' so rare informative words have more influence. [1]
deepDive.formula: P = (1/|ŷ|)·Σ max_{x∈y} cos(x̂,x),  R = (1/|y|)·Σ max_{x̂∈ŷ} cos(x̂,x)

deepDive.heading: Human Correlation Comparison
deepDive.body: In the original paper, BERTScore with DeBERTa-xlarge correlates substantially better with human quality judgments than BLEU and ROUGE-L across machine translation and summarisation benchmarks. For abstractive summarisation, the gap is particularly large because BLEU and ROUGE penalise paraphrases that humans find perfectly acceptable. Learned neural metrics like COMET or GPT-4-as-judge achieve even higher correlation but require running a full model at evaluation time, which is slower and more expensive. [1]

deepDive.heading: Limitations and When ROUGE Is Still Preferred
deepDive.body: BERTScore is slower than ROUGE because it requires a BERT forward pass on every candidate-reference pair. Absolute scores are not intuitive — a score of 0.90 vs 0.95 is hard to interpret without context, unlike BLEU. The score also depends on which model and layer you use, so you must always report those details for reproducibility. For extractive summarisation, where the goal is lexical fidelity to the source, ROUGE-2 remains the standard. Use BERTScore for abstractive generation, paraphrase evaluation, or whenever semantic equivalence matters more than word overlap. [1]

youtubeLabel: BERTScore: Evaluating Text Generation with BERT
takeaways: P=(1/|ŷ|)Σmax cos(x̂,x), R=(1/|y|)Σmax cos(x̂,x) — greedy token-level cosine matching. [1]
Contextual embeddings handle synonyms and paraphrases that BLEU/ROUGE penalise. [1]
DeBERTa-xlarge-mnli gives best human correlation for English evaluation. [1]
BERTScore F1 correlates significantly better with human judgments than BLEU/ROUGE. [1]
IDF weighting: rare tokens weighted higher — reduces effect of stopwords. [1]

edgeCases.q: How does BERTScore handle multilingual evaluation?
edgeCases.a: For multilingual evaluation: use mBERT (multilingual BERT) or XLM-RoBERTa as the backbone. The bert_score library supports a language parameter that selects the recommended model for that language. Cross-lingual BERTScore: computing BERTScore between an English candidate and a German reference using a multilingual model — directly evaluating semantic equivalence across languages. This is used for zero-shot MT evaluation (no reference in the target language needed). Limitation: BERTScore quality degrades for low-resource languages if the backbone model was not well pre-trained on that language — check the backbone model's language coverage before using for rare languages.

edgeCases.q: Should BERTScore's F1 be treated as an absolute quality score?
edgeCases.a: No. BERTScore F1 values are typically in a narrow range (0.80–0.95 for English text with BERT-base) because all English sentences share common BERT subspace structure. The absolute values are not intuitive: F1=0.85 does not mean '85% quality.' BERTScore is most useful as a relative metric for comparing systems on the same task and test set. When reporting: always specify the backbone model, layer, IDF weighting, and language — different configurations give different absolute ranges. For interpretability: report the relative improvement in BERTScore F1 between systems (e.g., '+0.03 F1') rather than absolute values, alongside the range on the benchmark.

sources.label: Zhang et al. (2020) — BERTScore: Evaluating Text Generation with BERT, ICLR 2020 arXiv:1904.09675
